analysis:
- High-performing heuristics share the same skeleton: Pareto-prefilter → cheap proxy scoring (box-volume ± scalarization) → O(k)-proportional shortlist → greedy/CELF selection with cached singleton HVs → bounded 1-for-1 local swaps → deterministic padding/tie-breaks.
- Strength differences come from where work is spent: best methods minimize exact HV calls by (a) tight shortlists, (b) good inexpensive proxies to rank candidates, and (c) robust lazy (CELF) evaluation to avoid recalculation. Heuristics 1–3 show complementary tradeoffs: H1 does exact greedy on a tiny shortlist then swaps (very HV-efficient); H3 emphasizes better scoring vectors and CELF (fewer wasted HVs).
- Effective micro-optimizations observed: precompute ref − points and rect_vol, cache singleton HVs, selectively cache pair HV only when shortlist is small, use stable deterministic ordering, and limit swap iterations. These yield big wall-time and call-count wins.
- Common inefficiencies in weaker variants: frequent full-stack HV recomputations, large shortlists that explode HV calls, redundant array copies/resizing, per-candidate Python loops without vectorized prefiltering, and unnecessary RNG shuffles that add nondeterministic overhead or extra work.
- Pruning and budgets matter: using cheap upper bounds (box-volume or singleton HV) to skip recomputations, and enforcing an HV-call budget with graceful fallbacks, preserves quality under time limits.
- Data-reuse opportunities underused in weaker heuristics: incremental HV updates (reuse previous computations rather than recomputing full stack), memory views instead of vstack copies, and reusing timestamped marginal gains (CELF) more aggressively.
- Reproducibility: deterministic seeds, stable sorts, and small tie-breaking rules are crucial for predictable selection and easier debugging/tuning.
experience: Prioritize reducing exact HV calls: build a high-precision O(k) shortlist with cheap proxies, cache singleton (and small pairwise) HVs, use CELF lazy updates and box‑bound pruning, then run a tiny deterministic swap pass. Add an explicit HV-call budget with graceful fallback approximations and vectorized nondomination/precomputation to get consistent, fast, and high-quality k‑subsets.
current_reflection: Prioritize reducing exact HV calls: build a high-precision O(k) shortlist with cheap proxies, cache singleton (and small pairwise) HVs, use CELF lazy updates and box‑bound pruning, then run a tiny deterministic swap pass. Add an explicit HV-call budget with graceful fallback approximations and vectorized nondomination/precomputation to get consistent, fast, and high-quality k‑subsets.
good_reflections: Prefilter dominated points, approximate HVC with few weight vectors to shortlist candidates, then perform a local search using cached incremental HVC deltas. Minimize Python-side HV calls by batching and avoid nested HV recomputations. Make the algorithm deterministic (seeded) and ensure it always returns exactly k points.

Design next heuristics by enforcing an O(k) shortlist, always cache singleton HVs, use CELF lazy evaluation with timestamps, apply cheap box-volume upper bounds to prune candidates, enable pairwise caching only for very small shortlists, cap HV calls and swap iterations, and prefer deterministic stable ordering. These focused changes yield most HV-quality gain per expensive HV call.
bad_reflections: Favor deterministic Pareto prefilter, vectorized box+scalar proxies to build an O(k) shortlist, precompute singleton HVs, run CELF lazy greedy with timestamps, conditionally cache pairs for small shortlists, apply tight cheap upper bounds to prune, cap HV calls, and use efficient numpy masks/sets. These deliver high-quality hypervolume with far fewer expensive HV evaluations.

For the next generation, combine: adaptive shortlist sizing (scale with pareto size and k), CELF lazy greedy with cached singletons + selective pairwise HVs, and tight upper-bound pruning (box-vol or scalar HVC) to avoid useless HV calls. Add an HV-call budget, incremental HV updates, and prioritize swap candidates via proxy gains or diversity. Vectorize dominance checks and weight computations; keep all randomness seeded and tie-break deterministically.

Always budget and minimize HV calls: prioritize vectorized proxies for aggressive pruning, cache singleton HVs, compute pairwise HVs only for very small shortlists, and use CELF lazy timestamps to amortize updates. Favor deterministic, O(N log N) shortlist construction and bounded, incremental swaps. Optimize hot loops with numpy and sets to avoid Python overhead; add an explicit HV-call budget and conservative upper bounds to maximize runtime-quality trade-offs.
comprehensive_reflection:
- Keywords: shortlist O(k), cached singleton HVs, CELF lazy eval, box-volume upper bounds, pairwise caching (small), HV-call budget, deterministic swaps, vectorized prefilter
- Advice: build an O(k) shortlist with vectorized dominance/box or few-weight proxies, precompute singleton HVCs, run CELF with timestamps and cheap upper‑bound pruning, enable pairwise caching only for very small shortlists, cap HV calls and fallback to approximations, finish with a tiny deterministic swap pass
- Avoid: repeated Python-side HV calls, nested recomputations, large pairwise caches, nondeterministic tie-breaking
- Explanation: minimizes expensive HV evaluations while delivering consistent, high-quality k-subsets and predictable runtime trade-offs
